title: System Design from Scratch
tagline: 44 lessons. 9 phases. From a single server to globally distributed systems — build the mental models behind scalable architecture.
description: A ground-up course on designing large-scale systems: how to estimate load, store and cache data, scale across machines, reason about consistency, and stitch services together with queues and APIs. Each phase pairs the concept with runnable code — a consistent hashing ring, an LRU cache, a token-bucket rate limiter — so the abstractions stay concrete, and ends in capstone designs of real systems.

phases.name: Foundations
phases.desc: Learn what system design actually is, how to turn vague requirements into numbers, and the framework you use to attack any design problem.

lessons.name: What Is System Design
lessons.summary: Define system design as the practice of trading off scalability, availability, latency, consistency, and cost, and map the building blocks you will use throughout the course.

lessons.name: Requirements & Tradeoffs
lessons.summary: Separate functional from non-functional requirements and turn fuzzy goals like 'fast' and 'reliable' into measurable SLAs.

lessons.name: Back-of-the-Envelope Estimation
lessons.summary: Write a Python estimator that turns daily active users into QPS, storage, and bandwidth numbers you can defend in a design review.
lessons.keywords: QPS · storage estimate · bandwidth · powers of two

lessons.name: The Design Framework
lessons.summary: Apply a repeatable 6-step framework — requirements, estimation, API, data model, high-level design, deep dive — to structure any design problem.
lessons.keywords: framework · high-level design · deep dive · bottlenecks

phases.name: Networking & APIs
phases.desc: Understand how requests find your servers and how clients talk to them: DNS, load balancers, proxies, and the API styles you choose between.

lessons.name: DNS & Domain Resolution
lessons.summary: Trace a domain name from browser to IP through recursive resolvers, and use DNS records for routing and failover.

lessons.name: Load Balancing
lessons.summary: Build a load balancer in Python that distributes requests across backends with round-robin, least-connections, and weighted strategies.
lessons.keywords: load balancer · round-robin · health check · L4 vs L7

lessons.name: Proxies & API Gateways
lessons.summary: Distinguish forward proxies, reverse proxies, and API gateways, and place each correctly in a request path.
lessons.keywords: reverse proxy · API gateway · TLS termination · cross-cutting

lessons.name: API Design: REST, gRPC, GraphQL
lessons.summary: Implement the same endpoint three ways and compare REST, gRPC, and GraphQL on payload size, coupling, and tooling.

lessons.name: Realtime: WebSockets & Polling
lessons.summary: Compare short polling, long polling, SSE, and WebSockets by building a live counter and measuring each approach's overhead.

phases.name: Data Storage
phases.desc: Pick the right store for the job: relational vs non-relational models, how indexes make queries fast, what ACID guarantees, and where blobs live.

lessons.name: SQL vs NoSQL
lessons.summary: Map workloads to data models — relational, key-value, document, wide-column, graph — and justify the choice with access patterns.

lessons.name: Indexing & Query Performance
lessons.summary: Create B-tree indexes in SQLite, read query plans with EXPLAIN, and measure the speedup from indexing a million-row table.
lessons.keywords: B-tree · EXPLAIN · composite index · full scan

lessons.name: ACID & Transactions
lessons.summary: Explain atomicity, consistency, isolation, and durability and reproduce a dirty-read and lost-update with isolation levels.

lessons.name: Data Modeling & Schema Design
lessons.summary: Model a real domain with normalized tables, then denormalize deliberately for read-heavy access and justify each tradeoff.
lessons.keywords: normalization · denormalization · foreign keys · star schema

lessons.name: Object & Blob Storage
lessons.summary: Explain why large files belong in object stores like S3 rather than databases, and design upload/download paths with presigned URLs.
lessons.keywords: object storage · S3 · presigned URL · CDN origin

phases.name: Caching & CDNs
phases.desc: Make reads fast and cheap: where to put a cache, how to keep it fresh, which entries to evict, and how CDNs push content to the edge.

lessons.name: Why Cache
lessons.summary: Quantify how caching cuts latency and load, and identify every layer of a stack where a cache can live.
lessons.keywords: cache hit ratio · latency · locality · cache layers

lessons.name: Caching Strategies
lessons.summary: Implement cache-aside, write-through, and write-back in Python and reason about staleness and consistency for each.

lessons.name: Eviction Policies & LRU
lessons.summary: Build an O(1) LRU cache with a hash map and doubly linked list, then compare it against LFU and FIFO on a real trace.
lessons.keywords: LRU · LFU · eviction · doubly linked list

lessons.name: Distributed Caching with Redis
lessons.summary: Use Redis as a shared cache, handle the thundering-herd and stampede problems, and reason about cache cluster topology.

lessons.name: CDNs & Edge Caching
lessons.summary: Explain how CDNs use anycast and edge PoPs to serve content near users, and design cache-control headers for correct invalidation.

phases.name: Scaling & Partitioning
phases.desc: Grow past one machine: replicate for reads, shard for writes, keep services stateless, and distribute load evenly with consistent hashing.

lessons.name: Vertical vs Horizontal Scaling
lessons.summary: Compare scaling up versus scaling out, and identify when each hits a wall in cost, availability, or complexity.
lessons.keywords: scale up · scale out · single point of failure · elasticity

lessons.name: Database Replication
lessons.summary: Explain single-leader, multi-leader, and leaderless replication and the read-after-write and replication-lag problems each creates.

lessons.name: Sharding & Partitioning
lessons.summary: Partition data by range and by hash in Python, expose the hot-shard problem, and design a partition key that spreads load.
lessons.keywords: sharding · partition key · hot shard · rebalancing

lessons.name: Consistent Hashing
lessons.summary: Build a consistent hashing ring with virtual nodes and measure how few keys move when a node joins or leaves.
lessons.keywords: consistent hashing · hash ring · virtual nodes · key movement

lessons.name: Stateless Services & Sessions
lessons.summary: Move session state out of app servers so any instance can handle any request, and compare sticky sessions to a shared session store.
lessons.keywords: stateless · sticky sessions · JWT · session store

phases.name: Distributed Systems Theory
phases.desc: The hard part: what CAP really says, the spectrum of consistency models, how nodes agree with consensus, and why clocks lie.

lessons.name: The CAP Theorem
lessons.summary: State CAP precisely — that during a partition you choose consistency or availability — and apply it to real datastore choices.

lessons.name: Consistency Models
lessons.summary: Order the consistency spectrum from strong to eventual and pick the weakest model that still satisfies the requirements.

lessons.name: Consensus & Raft
lessons.summary: Simulate Raft leader election and log replication in Python to see how a cluster agrees on a single value despite failures.

lessons.name: Time, Clocks & Ordering
lessons.summary: Implement Lamport and vector clocks to order events across machines without trusting wall-clock time.
lessons.keywords: Lamport clock · vector clock · happens-before · causality

lessons.name: Leader Election & Coordination
lessons.summary: Use coordination services like ZooKeeper/etcd for leader election, locks, and config, and reason about split-brain.

phases.name: Async & Messaging
phases.desc: Decouple producers from consumers: queues for work, pub/sub for fan-out, logs like Kafka for replay, and how to handle duplicates.

lessons.name: Message Queues
lessons.summary: Build a producer/consumer queue with a worker pool in Python and absorb a traffic spike without dropping work.

lessons.name: Pub/Sub & Event-Driven Design
lessons.summary: Implement a topic-based pub/sub broker so one event fans out to many independent subscribers.

lessons.name: Kafka & Log-Based Streaming
lessons.summary: Explain the partitioned append-only log model behind Kafka, consumer groups, offsets, and why replay changes architecture.
lessons.keywords: Kafka · partitioned log · consumer group · offset

lessons.name: Stream Processing
lessons.summary: Contrast batch and stream processing and design windowed aggregations over an unbounded event stream.
lessons.keywords: stream processing · windowing · watermark · batch vs stream

lessons.name: Idempotency & Delivery Guarantees
lessons.summary: Implement idempotent consumers with dedup keys and reason about at-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once delivery.

phases.name: Reliability & Observability
phases.desc: Keep systems up and visible: limit abusive traffic, fail gracefully, design for redundancy, and measure everything against SLOs.

lessons.name: Rate Limiting
lessons.summary: Implement token-bucket and sliding-window rate limiters and choose limits that protect a service without harming real users.
lessons.keywords: rate limiting · token bucket · sliding window · 429

lessons.name: Circuit Breakers & Retries
lessons.summary: Build a circuit breaker with retry-and-backoff and jitter, and show how naive retries cause a retry storm.
lessons.keywords: circuit breaker · exponential backoff · jitter · retry storm

lessons.name: Fault Tolerance & Redundancy
lessons.summary: Eliminate single points of failure with redundancy, failover, and multi-AZ deployment, and compute availability from components.

lessons.name: Monitoring & Observability
lessons.summary: Instrument the three pillars — metrics, logs, traces — and design dashboards and alerts that catch problems before users do.

lessons.name: SLOs & Capacity Planning
lessons.summary: Compute SLIs, error budgets, and percentile latency from raw request logs and plan capacity for projected growth.

phases.name: Capstone Projects
phases.desc: Put it all together: full end-to-end designs of systems you have used, with estimation, data model, API, scaling plan, and tradeoffs.

lessons.name: Design a URL Shortener
lessons.summary: Design and prototype a URL shortener: key generation, redirect path, read-heavy caching, and analytics at scale.

lessons.name: Design a Rate Limiter Service
lessons.summary: Design a distributed rate limiter shared across many app servers using Redis, with correct counting under concurrency.
lessons.keywords: distributed rate limiter · Redis · atomicity · sliding log

lessons.name: Design a Twitter News Feed
lessons.summary: Design a social news feed and resolve the fan-out-on-write vs fan-out-on-read tradeoff for celebrity accounts.
lessons.keywords: news feed · fan-out · timeline · celebrity problem

lessons.name: Design a Chat System
lessons.summary: Design a realtime chat system with WebSocket gateways, message storage, presence, and delivery/read receipts.

lessons.name: Design a Video Streaming Platform
lessons.summary: Design a video platform: upload and transcoding pipeline, adaptive bitrate streaming, and CDN-backed delivery at scale.

glossary.means: The ability of a system to handle increased load by adding resources, ideally with throughput growing proportionally to resources added.
The fraction of time a system is operational and able to serve requests, usually expressed in nines (99.9% = ~8.7 hours of downtime per year).
The time to service a single request, measured end to end. Often reported as percentiles (p50, p99) rather than averages because tails matter.
The number of operations a system completes per unit time, e.g. requests per second (QPS). Distinct from latency: a batch system can have high throughput and high latency.
Queries per second — the request rate a service handles. Peak QPS, not average, drives capacity planning.
Service Level Agreement — a contractual guarantee about availability or latency, with penalties if missed. Built from internal SLOs and SLIs.
Service Level Objective — the target value for a metric (e.g. 99.9% of requests under 200ms) that the team commits to internally.
Service Level Indicator — the actual measured metric (e.g. the observed p99 latency) that an SLO is defined against.
A component that distributes incoming requests across multiple backend servers using a strategy like round-robin or least-connections, often with health checks.
A proxy that sits in front of backend servers and forwards client requests to them, often handling TLS termination, caching, and load balancing.
A reverse proxy specialized for APIs that centralizes cross-cutting concerns: authentication, rate limiting, routing, and request aggregation.
Domain Name System — the distributed directory that resolves human-readable domain names to IP addresses through a hierarchy of recursive and authoritative servers.
Content Delivery Network — a geographically distributed set of edge caches that serve static (and sometimes dynamic) content close to users via anycast routing.
A high-speed store holding copies of data so future reads avoid a slower source. Defined by its eviction policy, size, and consistency with the source of truth.
The fraction of requests served from cache rather than the origin. A small ratio increase can dramatically reduce backend load.
A caching pattern where the application checks the cache, and on a miss reads the database and populates the cache itself. Also called lazy loading.
Least Recently Used — an eviction policy that discards the entry not accessed for the longest time, typically implemented with a hash map plus a doubly linked list for O(1) operations.
Keeping copies of data on multiple nodes for availability and read scaling. Variants include single-leader, multi-leader, and leaderless.
The delay between a write committing on the leader and appearing on a follower. Causes read-after-write anomalies if a client reads from a stale replica.
Partitioning a dataset across multiple nodes so each holds a subset, enabling writes and storage to scale horizontally. Requires choosing a partition key.
The attribute used to decide which shard a record lives on. A poor choice creates hot shards; a good one spreads load evenly.
A partition that receives disproportionate traffic because of a skewed partition key (e.g. sharding by country when one country dominates).
A hashing scheme that maps keys and nodes onto a ring so that adding or removing a node moves only a small fraction of keys, not the whole keyspace.
Multiple positions on a consistent hashing ring assigned to one physical node, used to smooth out uneven key distribution.
During a network partition a distributed system must choose between consistency and availability; partition tolerance is non-negotiable, so the real choice is CP vs AP.
A model where every read returns the most recent committed write. Linearizability is the strongest form. Costs latency and availability under partitions.
A model where replicas converge to the same value given no new writes, but reads may temporarily return stale data. Common in AP systems.
The strongest single-object consistency: operations appear to take effect instantaneously in some real-time order consistent with their actual timing.
The problem of getting multiple nodes to agree on a single value despite failures. Solved by protocols like Raft and Paxos using quorums.
A consensus algorithm built around an elected leader that replicates an append-only log to followers, committing entries once a quorum acknowledges them.
The minimum number of nodes that must respond for an operation to succeed. With N nodes, a write/read quorum of W+R > N guarantees overlap.
A counter that increments on each event and on message receipt takes the max of local and received values, establishing a partial happens-before order.
An array of logical counters, one per node, that can detect whether two events are causally ordered or concurrent.
A failure where a partition causes two nodes to each believe they are leader, leading to divergent writes. Prevented with quorums and fencing.
A component that holds messages between producers and consumers, decoupling them in time and absorbing load spikes via backpressure.
Publish/subscribe — a messaging pattern where publishers send to a topic and every subscriber receives a copy, enabling fan-out.
A distributed, partitioned, append-only commit log. Consumers track their own offset, enabling replay and multiple independent reads of the same stream.
A property where performing an operation multiple times has the same effect as performing it once, essential for safe retries in at-least-once delivery.
A delivery guarantee where every message is delivered, but possibly more than once. Combined with idempotent consumers to achieve effective exactly-once.
A mechanism by which a slow consumer signals upstream to reduce the rate of incoming work, preventing unbounded queue growth and memory exhaustion.
Restricting how many requests a client may make in a window to protect a service. Common algorithms: token bucket, leaky bucket, sliding window.
A rate-limiting algorithm where tokens refill at a fixed rate up to a capacity; each request consumes a token, allowing bursts up to the bucket size.
A pattern that trips open after repeated failures to a dependency, failing fast instead of piling up calls, then probes periodically to recover.
A retry strategy where the wait between attempts grows exponentially (often with random jitter) to avoid synchronized retry storms.
A component whose failure takes down the whole system. Eliminated through redundancy and failover.
The amount of unreliability permitted by an SLO (e.g. 0.1% of requests may fail). Spending it gates risky releases.
The latency value below which 99% of requests fall — i.e. the slowest 1%. Tail latency matters because one slow dependency can dominate a fan-out request.
A service that keeps no client-specific state between requests, so any instance can serve any request and scaling out is trivial.
A client-supplied unique identifier attached to a request so the server can detect and ignore duplicate submissions of the same operation.
When a single logical write triggers multiple physical writes (indexes, replicas, fan-out), increasing load disproportionately.
A single key, partition, or node receiving disproportionate traffic, often the limiting factor in an otherwise well-distributed system.
